secrets.context: Default hardcoded WiFi access point password for fallback AP mode

impact.description: Anyone with network access to the ESP32 in AP mode can connect using this password. This is used when the device cannot connect to configured WiFi networks.
impact.affected_systems: All ESP32_ZACUS devices deployed with this firmware
attack_scenario: 1. Device cannot connect to primary WiFi (e.g., traveling, network down)
2. Device enters fallback AP mode with SSID="Freenove-Setup", password="mascarade"
3. Attacker connects to AP using known credentials
4. Attacker gains access to all device APIs and controls (unlock, camera, media, etc.)
5. No authentication required on any API endpoints (see CRITICAL_002)

cryptanalysis.compliance_failure: Fails minimum password complexity requirements (NIST SP 800-132)

secrets.production_context: Test/Development value, but embedded in production firmware

steps: 1. Remove all hardcoded passwords from source code
2. Store AP password in encrypted NVS (Non-Volatile Storage) using ESP32's NVS partition
3. Generate unique random password during first boot using device MAC address + entropy
4. Implement secure provisioning via QR code or BLE for changing credentials
5. Never allow AP mode without authentication after initial setup
6. Re-release firmware with empty default password (require manual setup)

locations.context: Embedded WiFi configuration for local network

secrets.context: Default WiFi network SSID paired with hardcoded password

impact.description: The SSID 'Les cils' reveals network identity in firmware. Combined with known password 'mascarade', this enables complete WiFi hijacking.
impact.affected_systems: Any ESP32_ZACUS device in 'local' mode or attempting to connect to this network
attack_vector: 1. Attacker decompiles firmware and finds SSID and password
2. Creates rogue WiFi AP with identical SSID and password
3. Performs MITM attack by disconnecting original network
4. Dev devices or testing devices connect to rogue AP
5. All device communications can be intercepted/modified

secrets.production_context: Appears to be developer's home network (French name). Should NOT be in production firmware.

steps: 1. Remove 'Les cils' SSID from firmware entirely
2. Implement provisioning mode where user provides WiFi credentials at setup
3. Store user-provided credentials in encrypted NVS
4. Never hardcode any WiFi SSID in firmware
5. Document expected setup flow for users

secrets.context: Backup WiFi configuration for testing purposes

impact.description: Identical to local_ssid but used as fallback for testing. Same credentials (Les cils / mascarade) mean attacker has two attack vectors.
impact.affected_systems: All devices in testing mode or after failed primary WiFi connection

secrets.production_context: Test credentials should not be in production firmware at all

steps: 1. Remove test_ssid and test_password entirely from production firmware
2. Keep these only in development/debug builds (conditional compilation)
3. Use build flags to distinguish: #ifdef FIRMWARE_DEBUG_BUILD

locations.context: Access Point SSID in embedded config

secrets.context: Access Point (hotspot) name broadcast when device is in AP mode

impact.description: The SSID 'Freenove-Setup' is hardcoded and publicly visible in WiFi networks. While the AP SSID alone is informational, combined with the known password 'mascarade', it enables unauthorized access.
impact.affected_systems: All ESP32_ZACUS devices in fallback AP mode
impact.visibility: Visible to anyone with a WiFi scanner in proximity

secrets.production_context: SHOULD be customized per device but currently hardcoded

steps: 1. Generate unique AP SSID per device (e.g., 'Freenove-XXXXXX' where XXXXXX = last 6 digits of MAC)
2. Update at runtime based on device MAC address
3. Make AP password strong and random (64 chars, mixed case, symbols)
4. Consider requiring QR code scan for AP credentials instead of hardcoding

secrets.cwe: CWE-200: Exposure of Sensitive Information to an Unauthorized Actor

secrets.context: Access Point (fallback AP mode) has NO password in default configuration

impact.description: The AP mode broadcasts 'Freenove-Setup' SSID with NO password. This means ANYONE within WiFi range can connect to the device without any authentication.
impact.affected_systems: All ESP32_ZACUS devices that fall back to AP mode
attack_scenario: 1. Developer's device falls back to AP mode (no primary network available)
2. Any attacker nearby opens WiFi settings
4. Attacker is now connected to device with access to ALL APIs
5. Can control camera, media, audio, unlock, unlock settings, etc.
6. All protected by ZERO authentication (see CRITICAL_002)

secrets.combined_risk: When combined with unauthenticated API endpoints, this creates an OPEN SYSTEM vulnerability
secrets.production_context: CRITICAL SECURITY FLAW - device is essentially completely open when in AP mode

steps: 1. Generate strong random password during first boot (minimum 16 chars)
2. Store password in NVS encrypted storage
3. Display password only during setup (QR code or serial output)
4. Never allow open AP without explicit configuration
5. When no password provided, generate one automatically
6. Document setup process clearly for users

secrets.cwe: CWE-306: Missing Authentication for Critical Function

secrets.context: mDNS hostname for device discovery on network

impact.description: While not a secret in the traditional sense, predictable hostname enables easier device discovery and fingerprinting. Combined with other factors, helps attackers identify and target devices.
impact.affected_systems: All devices on local network can see this hostname

secrets.production_context: Informational value only, but could be made unique per device

steps: 1. Generate unique hostname per device (e.g., 'zacus-XXXXXX' where XXXXXX = MAC suffix)
2. Allow customization via API after authentication
3. Store in NVS if changed

secrets.cwe: CWE-200: Exposure of Sensitive Information to an Unauthorized Actor

locations.context: Token loading from NVS or generation

secrets.context: API authentication token stored in RAM and NVS (Preferences)

impact.description: Bearer tokens are stored in process RAM and also persisted in NVS storage. While better than hardcoded values, they could be extracted via RAM dumps or NVS partition analysis.
impact.vulnerability: Tokens not cleared from memory after use (can be memory-dumped)
attack_vector: 1. Physical access to device + UART interface
2. Dump process memory via debugger interface
3. Search for 'Bearer ' string patterns in memory
4. Extract token and use for API access
5. Or read raw NVS partition if not encrypted

secrets.production_context: This is an IMPLEMENTATION, not a hardcoded secret. The approach is reasonable but has minor vulnerabilities.

steps: 1. Implement secure token storage with encryption (use ESP32 NVS encryption)
2. Clear token from RAM immediately after use
3. Consider implementing token rotation with expiration times
4. Use volatile variables and memset() to clear sensitive data
5. Consider RNG for token generation (currently may use predictable sources)

vulnerability_summary.overall_risk: CRITICAL - Production deployment is NOT RECOMMENDED until CRITICAL issues are resolved

issues: Fallback AP with known credentials or no password
No encryption for credentials in NVS
Bearer token in plain text in memory

phase_1_immediate: SECRET_002: Remove 'Les cils' SSID from firmware
SECRET_005: Generate strong random AP password at boot time
phase_2_urgent: SECRET_004: Make AP SSID unique per device (use MAC address)
Implement NVS encryption for all credentials
phase_3_important: SECRET_006: Generate unique hostname per device
SECRET_007: Implement secure token storage with encryption

ui_freenove_allinone/src/storage_manager.action: Remove hardcoded WiFi credentials from embedded JSON

ui_freenove_allinone/include/runtime/run.action: Keep ap_default_ssid hardcoded, but make ap_default_password empty or generate at runtime

ui_freenove_allinone/src/runtime/runtime.action: Update default values and add credential generation from NVS

immediate_actions: ✅ Stop deploying firmware to production immediately until Phase 1 is complete
✅ Audit all deployed devices and change default credentials manually
✅ Issue security notice to all device holders
architecture_recommendations: Implement provisioning mode requiring QR code or BLE for credentials setup
Use ESP32's built-in secure boot and flash encryption features
Implement certificate-based device identification instead of hardcoded values
Add DTLS/TLS for encrypted API communication
Implement firmware signing and secure updates
testing: Add security tests to CI/CD checking for hardcoded passwords
Implement secret scanning in git (e.g., git-secrets, TruffleHog)
Add penetration testing to release process

audit_notes.scan_coverage: Complete - all C++, Python, JSON, YAML files scanned
audit_notes.false_positives: None identified. All findings are actual hardcoded values.
related_vulnerabilities: CWE-306: Missing Authentication for Critical Function (all API endpoints unprotected)
references: SECURITY_AUDIT_FR.md - French security audit with remediation code

compliance_status.NIST_SP_800_132: FAIL - password doesn't meet minimum complexity

conclusion.estimated_severity_reduction: From CRITICAL to LOW after implementing all phases
conclusion.sign_off_required: Security review before deployment to production
